our_argument_summary: WIN-013 is classified as 'Misleading' because the dome model claims both a positive gravity detection at Mohe (WIN-011) and a null SG result at Membach (WIN-013) as simultaneous confirmations. Without advance specification of which locations should show effects vs. nulls, any outcome confirms the model — the hallmark of unfalsifiability. Our text now also includes the instrumental hierarchy argument (SGs are ~1,000× more precise than the LaCoste-Romberg spring gravimeter).

relevant_files: Domain 7 in monitor.py handles WIN-012 κ coupling only (per Cycle 1 analysis)
dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: Domain 7 in monitor.py validates the EM-gravity coupling constant (WIN-012) using hardcoded values: 10.9 nT / 6.5 μGal = 1.67 nT/μGal. WIN-013 (Membach SG null) has no validation logic in monitor.py — no domain, no fetch, no comparison. The 0.0 μGal value is stated only on the dome website.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: WIN-013 is not computed or validated at all. The dome website simply states 'Van Camp 1999. 0.0 μGal. Confirms electromagnetic, not gravitational.' There is no code that checks, fetches, or validates this claim.
data_sources_used: None — no data is fetched for WIN-013
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: The dome's framing is a heads-I-win-tails-you-lose escape: positive detection (WIN-011) proves EM-gravity coupling; null detection (WIN-013) proves 'electromagnetic, not gravitational.' Both outcomes confirm the model.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: Potential monitoring tag error — see holes_found below.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: The Cycle 1 review's own dome_code_analysis concluded 'WIN-013 is NOT validated anywhere in the monitor code' and data_sources_used was 'None.' Domain 7 in monitor.py handles WIN-012's coupling constant only, not WIN-013. Yet the current wins.json tag says 'hardcoded'. Since there is no domain, no fetch, no comparison logic for this WIN, the correct tag is 'none' — consistent with WIN-014's tag and with the Cycle 1 review's own analysis. The 'hardcoded' tag appears to be an error, possibly from grouping WIN-013 with the WIN-011/012 cluster which IS Domain 7.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: The SG null result is a real empirical finding. Standard physics does not predict eclipse gravity anomalies (the Allais effect is not accepted). The dome model's interpretation of the null as confirming 'electromagnetic, not gravitational' is dome-specific framing, not a relabeling of any standard physics explanation.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: Van Camp et al.'s 1999 eclipse gravimetry study was published in 2001 (DOI: 10.1023/A:1013940928637). The dome model adopted this known null result and retroactively labeled it a confirmed prediction. The dome site cites 'Van Camp 1999' (the eclipse year, not the publication year), which further obscures the timeline.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: No dome equation predicts which eclipse observation sites should yield null results vs. positive detections. The 0.0 μGal value is simply adopted from Van Camp et al. — it is not derived from disc radius, firmament height, or any dome-specific parameter. The dome's interpretation ('electromagnetic, not gravitational') is post-hoc framing, not a geometric derivation.

holes_found.description: Monitoring tag mismatch: wins.json has monitoring='hardcoded' for WIN-013, but the Cycle 1 curmudgeon review's own dome_code_analysis explicitly states 'WIN-013 is NOT validated anywhere in the monitor code' and 'data_sources_used: None.' This contradicts the 'hardcoded' tag. Domain 7 in monitor.py validates only WIN-012's coupling constant (κ = 10.9/6.5), not WIN-013's null result. Compare: WIN-014 (China SG null, same logical structure) correctly has monitoring='none'. The WIN-013 tag should be 'none' for consistency and accuracy.
holes_found.recommendation: Change WIN-013 code_analysis.monitoring from 'hardcoded' to 'none' in wins.json. This makes it consistent with WIN-014 and with the Cycle 1 review's own analysis. The Part 4.6 counts will auto-recompute at build time.

holes_found.description: The dome's specific 'electromagnetic, not gravitational' argument is not directly engaged. The dome claims the SG null proves the eclipse effect is electromagnetic (which SGs can't detect), not gravitational (which SGs would detect). Our rebuttal focuses on the logical contradiction of claiming opposite outcomes, but doesn't address why the 'electromagnetic' escape fails on its own terms: (a) If the effect is purely electromagnetic, the Mohe spring gravimeter should not have detected it either — spring gravimeters measure gravity, not EM fields. A LaCoste-Romberg D responds to mass displacement, not magnetic flux. (b) WIN-012 explicitly derives a 'gravity coupling constant' from the Mohe measurement, presupposing a gravitational signal. The dome cannot simultaneously call the Mohe signal 'electromagnetic' (to explain the Membach null) and 'gravitational' (to derive κ).
holes_found.recommendation: Add a sentence to WIN-013 detail_evidence addressing the electromagnetic escape: 'The dome's claim that the null proves the effect is electromagnetic rather than gravitational fails because (a) the Mohe spring gravimeter that WIN-011 relies on also cannot detect electromagnetic effects — it measures gravitational acceleration only — and (b) WIN-012 explicitly treats the Mohe signal as gravitational to derive the coupling constant κ.'

holes_found.description: The Van Camp DOI (10.1023/A:1013940928637) resolves to a Kluwer/Springer publication. The dome cites 'Van Camp 1999' but the paper was published in 2001. Our text correctly says 'Van Camp et al., 2001' which is good. However, the Cycle 1 review cited 'J. Geodetic Soc. Japan 47(1), 387-392, 2001' while the DOI prefix (10.1023) is Kluwer Academic — these should be verified as the same paper. The DOI may point to a reprint or proceedings volume rather than the primary publication.
holes_found.recommendation: Verify that DOI 10.1023/A:1013940928637 resolves to the correct Van Camp eclipse gravimetry paper. If it's a proceedings version, note the primary journal reference. Low priority — the content is correct regardless of which venue the DOI resolves to.

holes_found.description: The detail_evidence mentions SGs have '~1,000× greater sensitivity' than the LaCoste-Romberg. This is approximately correct (SGs achieve ~0.01 μGal noise floors vs. ~2-3 μGal for spring gravimeters), but the factor depends on integration time and conditions. Van Camp et al. report a 2.0 μGal noise floor over 24 hours for the SG, while the Mohe uncertainty was 2.7 μGal per reading. The actual sensitivity advantage for short-period signals during an eclipse is more like 100-300× than 1,000×. A dome defender could challenge the '1,000×' figure.
holes_found.recommendation: Consider softening to 'orders of magnitude more precise' or specifying 'nanogal-level long-term sensitivity vs. several-μGal uncertainty for the spring gravimeter.' Not critical — the qualitative argument (precise instrument beats crude one) is unaffected.

stronger_arguments.description: Electromagnetic escape self-defeat: The dome's own framing ('electromagnetic, not gravitational') destroys WIN-011 and WIN-012 rather than saving WIN-013. If the eclipse effect is electromagnetic, then the Mohe spring gravimeter (which only measures gravity) could not have detected it, invalidating WIN-011. And if there's no gravitational signal, then κ = ΔB/Δg has a zero denominator, invalidating WIN-012. The dome is forced into a trilemma: (1) the effect is gravitational → WIN-013 should show it; (2) the effect is electromagnetic → WIN-011/012 are invalid; (3) it depends on location → specify which locations in advance or it's unfalsifiable. All three horns are fatal.
sources: Dome raw-text WIN-013: 'Confirms electromagnetic, not gravitational'
Dome raw-text WIN-012: 'coupling = 10.9 nT / 6.5 uGal = 1.67 nT/uGal'

stronger_arguments.description: The 2026 eclipse prediction (E-PRED-C) exposes the contradiction going forward. The dome's predictions page lists 'E-PRED-C — SG gravimeters 0.0 μGal' as a prediction for the August 12, 2026 eclipse. If the dome is predicting null SG results as the norm, then WIN-011's positive detection is the anomaly that needs explaining, not a confirmed prediction. The dome cannot use the same framework to predict both 0.0 μGal (future eclipses) and -6.5 μGal (past Mohe eclipse) without specifying what distinguishes them.

kernel_of_truth.description: The dome model correctly identifies a genuine scientific puzzle: why do some eclipse gravity measurements report marginal positive signals while more precise instruments consistently find null? The Allais effect has been investigated by serious physicists since 1954. Van Camp et al.'s 1999 study is real, peer-reviewed science, and its null result is accurately reported by the dome model. The dome also makes a defensible (if ultimately wrong) observation that different instrument types might respond differently to an eclipse — SGs are optimized for slow gravity changes, while spring gravimeters might conceivably respond to rapid perturbations that SGs filter out.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The instrument-response argument fails because: (a) Wang et al.'s 'anomaly' occurred at eclipse contacts (minutes-scale), well within SG bandwidth; (b) Van Camp's SG analysis specifically looked for eclipse-timescale signals and found nothing; (c) the dome doesn't make this instrument-selectivity argument — it instead claims the effect is 'electromagnetic,' which undermines WIN-011/012 as noted above. More fundamentally, even if the scientific puzzle is real, claiming both outcomes as confirmations without predictive specificity is textbook unfalsifiability.

fixes_applied: Citation misplacement FIXED: Van Camp citation now correctly attributed to WIN-013 null result, not WIN-011 positive claim
Geographic error FIXED (in WIN-011): Mohe now correctly listed as 'Heilongjiang Province, northeast China' instead of 'Tibet'
Value discrepancy FIXED (in WIN-011): detail_claim now says '−6.5 μGal' matching dome's own stated value
Instrumental hierarchy argument ADDED: detail_evidence now includes the 1,000× sensitivity comparison
Wang et al. citation ADDED: proper DOI link to Phys. Rev. D 62, 041101
fixes_not_applied: Sun et al. 2010 DOI verification (WIN-014) — not checked in this review cycle, but WIN-014 text acknowledges the citation uncertainty
Monitoring tag remains 'hardcoded' when Cycle 1 analysis concluded it should be 'none'
cycle_1_comparison.text_change_description: detail_evidence was substantially rewritten since Cycle 1 — new length 999 chars. The rewrite fixed the citation ordering, added Wang et al. DOI, added the instrumental hierarchy argument, and restructured the logical flow. All major Cycle 1 recommendations were addressed except the monitoring tag.

deeper_analysis:
Cycle 2 review of WIN-013 finds the argument substantially improved since Cycle 1. The three major Cycle 1 issues (citation misplacement, Mohe geography, value discrepancy) have all been fixed. The instrumental hierarchy argument was added as recommended. The verdict 'Misleading' remains correct — this is fundamentally about unfalsifiable claim structure rather than self-contradiction (WIN-012 correctly carries the 'Self-Contradicted' verdict for the κ denominator issue).

The main remaining improvement is engaging with the dome's specific 'electromagnetic, not gravitational' framing. Our current text treats the contradiction as purely logical (opposite outcomes = unfalsifiable), but the dome's escape attempt actually self-defeats: calling the effect electromagnetic destroys WIN-011 (spring gravimeters measure gravity, not EM) and WIN-012 (κ requires a gravitational denominator). This trilemma framing would make the argument more airtight against a sophisticated defender.

The monitoring tag error (hardcoded → should be none) is a data quality issue. The Cycle 1 review's own analysis correctly identified that monitor.py has no Domain or validation logic for WIN-013, yet the tag wasn't corrected. WIN-014 (identical logical structure — SG null for a different eclipse) correctly has monitoring='none'. This inconsistency should be fixed.

The 2026 eclipse prediction (E-PRED-C: SG gravimeters 0.0 μGal) is a valuable new angle not present in Cycle 1. If the dome's forward prediction is null SG results, this retrospectively undermines WIN-011's positive detection as the outlier, not WIN-013's null. This could be mentioned in the detail_evidence or detail_extra as it creates a testable forward prediction that, if confirmed (which it almost certainly will be), further weakens the WIN-011/012/013 cluster.

Overall: the argument has gone from 'good with fixable errors' (Cycle 1) to 'strong with refinement opportunities' (Cycle 2). No critical issues remain.

citations_verified: Wang et al., 2000, Phys. Rev. D 62, 041101 — DOI 10.1103/PhysRevD.62.041101 correctly cited in WIN-013 detail_evidence for Mohe spring gravimeter study
Van Camp et al., 2001 — DOI 10.1023/A:1013940928637 cited for Membach SG null; year correctly listed as 2001 (dome incorrectly says 1999)
citations_unchecked: Van Camp DOI 10.1023/A:1013940928637 — Kluwer prefix verified but full resolution to specific journal/proceedings volume not confirmed (no web access). Cycle 1 cited J. Geodetic Soc. Japan 47(1), which may differ from the DOI target.
